title: RuVector WASM SIMD Acceleration: Architecture and How It Works
content:
RuVector uses WebAssembly (WASM) with SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) instructions to accelerate vector similarity computations in the browser and Node.js environments where native Rust binaries are not available.

What SIMD Is and Why It Matters for Vector Search

SIMD is a CPU execution model where a single instruction operates on multiple data elements simultaneously. For vector databases, the hot path is computing similarity between floating-point vectors (cosine similarity, dot product, Euclidean distance). A 384-dimensional vector comparison requires 384 multiplications and additions. Without SIMD, these execute one at a time. With SIMD (AVX-512 on x86, NEON on ARM, or WASM SIMD128), 4-16 operations execute in a single clock cycle.

RuVector's WASM SIMD Implementation

RuVector compiles its Rust core (ruvector-core) to WebAssembly using wasm-pack with the SIMD128 target feature enabled. The resulting WASM module uses the 128-bit SIMD instructions available in all modern browsers (Chrome 91+, Firefox 89+, Safari 16.4+). The WASM runtime is deliberately small at 5.5 KB to minimize load time.

The implementation uses 8x loop unrolling with instruction-level parallelism (ILP). Each iteration of the inner loop processes 8 float values simultaneously rather than 1, maximising the utilisation of SIMD lanes.

Measured Performance (from benchmark analysis in this codebase)

Cosine Similarity (384 dimensions, 100,000 iterations):
- Baseline (no SIMD): 318.52ms total, 3.04 microseconds average
- SIMD optimized: 299.42ms total, 2.83 microseconds average
- Speedup: 1.07x
- Throughput: 333,977 operations per second
- Latency: p50=1.66 microseconds, p95=1.96 microseconds, p99=2.38 microseconds

Euclidean Distance (384 dimensions):
- Baseline: 200.72ms
- SIMD optimized: 167.96ms
- Speedup: 1.28x
- Throughput: 595,370 operations per second

Batch Operations (1,000 vectors):
- Total: 2,394ms
- Per batch: 2.394ms
- Throughput: 417,715 comparisons per second

Where the 2.49x-7.47x Numbers Come From

Those speedup figures refer specifically to the Flash Attention mechanism in the Agentic-Flow / Ruflo layer built on top of ruvector, not raw SIMD cosine similarity. Flash Attention restructures the attention computation to be memory-bandwidth-efficient. The 2.49x figure is for JavaScript runtime, the 7.47x figure is for NAPI (native Node.js bindings). The WASM SIMD layer underneath contributes 1.07x-1.28x to individual operations, while HNSW indexing contributes the large 125x-12,500x speedup for search (by avoiding brute-force comparison entirely).

Detection and Fallback

RuVector detects WASM SIMD availability at runtime. If SIMD128 is not supported (older browsers, restricted environments), it falls back to scalar WASM. If WASM itself is not available, Node.js uses the native napi-rs bindings which use AVX-512 or ARM NEON directly via Rust.

The fallback chain in the Ask-Ruvnet codebase is:
1. ONNX via @claude-flow/embeddings (approximately 55x faster than Xenova, approximately 930 embeds/sec)
2. RuVector native embedding service
3. @xenova/transformers (slower, model-download-required)
4. Hash-based fallback vectors (no semantic meaning, for debugging only)

title: RuVector WASM SIMD: Enabling and Configuring in Your Project
RuVector WASM SIMD: Vector Quantization and Memory Reduction
content:
RuVector implements multi-tier vector quantization to reduce memory footprint while maintaining search accuracy. This is critical for WASM deployments where browser memory is limited and for edge devices.

Quantization Levels and Trade-offs

Measured on 384-dimensional Float32 vectors:

Float32 (no compression):
- Size: 1.50 KB per vector
- Error: 0 (exact)
- Use case: Maximum accuracy, unlimited RAM

8-bit (int8) quantization:
- Size: 392 bytes per vector (25.5% of original, 4x reduction)
- Error: 0.000008 (negligible)
- Quantize throughput: 153,910 ops/sec
- Dequantize throughput: 170,248 ops/sec
- Use case: Production default, excellent accuracy/size balance

4-bit quantization:
- Size: 200 bytes per vector (13.0% of original, 7.5x reduction)
- Error: 0.002182 (acceptable for most retrieval tasks)
- Quantize throughput: 150,455 ops/sec
- Dequantize throughput: 353,846 ops/sec
- Use case: Memory-constrained environments, edge devices

Product Quantization (PQ):
- Size: 16 bytes per vector (1.0% of original, 100x reduction)
- Error: 0.600230 (high - use carefully)
- Use case: Approximate search only, massive scale (millions of vectors)

Memory Calculator for Your Deployment

| Vectors | Float32 | 8-bit | 4-bit | PQ |
|---------|---------|-------|-------|----|
| 10,000 | 15 MB | 3.8 MB | 1.95 MB | 160 KB |
| 100,000 | 150 MB | 38 MB | 19.5 MB | 1.6 MB |
| 1,000,000 | 1.5 GB | 375 MB | 195 MB | 16 MB |

For RuVector with PQ8 compression: 1M vectors = ~200 MB (vs 2 GB for Pinecone).

How to Configure Quantization

```rust
// Rust configuration
use ruvector_core::{VectorDB, QuantizationConfig};

let db = VectorDB::new(VectorDBConfig {
    dimensions: 384,
    distance_metric: DistanceMetric::Cosine,
    quantization: Some(QuantizationConfig {
        level: QuantizationLevel::Int8,  // or Int4, ProductQuantization
        compress_ratio: 4,
    }),
    ..Default::default()
});
```

```javascript
// JavaScript / Node.js
const { VectorDB } = require('ruvector');
const db = new VectorDB({
    dimensions: 384,
    distanceMetric: 'Cosine',
    compression: { level: 'int8' }  // 4x memory reduction
});
```

Browser WASM Memory Budget

Browsers impose limits on WASM memory. A typical 32-bit WASM module is limited to 4 GB of addressable memory, but practical limits are much lower:
- Chrome: 2 GB practical limit
- Firefox: 2 GB practical limit
- Safari: 1 GB practical limit on iOS

With 8-bit quantization, you can fit approximately 5 million 384-dim vectors in 2 GB. With product quantization, 125 million vectors fit in the same space.

RuVector's Adaptive Tiered Compression

The 2-32x compression figure in the documentation refers to the adaptive tiering system that automatically selects quantization level based on access frequency:
- Hot vectors (frequently accessed): Float32 in memory
- Warm vectors: 8-bit quantized in memory
- Cold vectors: 4-bit or PQ compressed, loaded on demand

This mirrors how database buffer pools work, but for vector data.

title: Building a Fully Private RAG Stack with RuVector: Architecture Overview
content:
A fully private RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) stack ensures that documents, queries, and generated answers never leave your infrastructure. RuVector enables this by providing all three required components locally: document embedding, vector storage and retrieval, and LLM inference.

Architecture Components

The three-component local RAG stack:

1. Embedding Model (local ONNX or RuVector native)
   - Converts documents and queries into vectors
   - Runs on CPU or GPU, no API calls
   - Model: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384 dimensions, 22MB)
   - Throughput: ~930 embeds/sec with ONNX

2. Vector Store (RuVector)
   - Stores and indexes document embeddings
   - HNSW index for fast approximate nearest neighbor search
   - 61 microsecond p50 search latency
   - Persistence via binary format with WAL

3. Local LLM (ruvllm or Ollama)
   - Generates answers from retrieved context
   - GGUF format models run on CPU/Metal/CUDA
   - No internet connection required

Data Flow

```
Document Ingestion:
  Document (PDF/TXT/MD)
    -> Text chunker (2000 chars, 200 overlap)
    -> Local embedding model (ONNX)
    -> 384-dim Float32Array
    -> RuVector HNSW index
    -> Binary persistence (.ruvector/vectors.bin)

Query Processing:
  User question
    -> Local embedding model (same ONNX model)
    -> 384-dim query vector
    -> RuVector HNSW search (top-k=5)
    -> Retrieved document chunks
    -> Context assembly
    -> Local LLM (Ollama/ruvllm)
    -> Streamed answer
    -> User
```

Privacy Guarantees

- No document content transmitted to external APIs
- No query text transmitted to external APIs
- No embedding API calls (OpenAI, Cohere, etc.)
- All computation on local hardware
- Network can be fully air-gapped after initial model download

This Codebase as Reference Implementation

The Ask-Ruvnet project demonstrates this architecture. The server (src/server/app.js) initializes:
- RuvectorStore (local HNSW vector DB with persistence)
- PostgresKnowledgeBase (ruvector-postgres extension, also local)
- TextChunker, QueryExpander, ReRanker, ContextCompressor
- Local ONNX embedding via @claude-flow/embeddings

When PostgreSQL is unavailable, it falls back fully to the file-based RuvectorStore, keeping everything local.

Hardware Requirements

Minimum for a useful private RAG system:
- RAM: 8 GB (4 GB for 7B Q4 model + 4 GB OS/app)
- Storage: 20 GB (model files + vector index)
- CPU: Any modern x86-64 or ARM64 (Apple M-series ideal)

Recommended:
- RAM: 32 GB (run 13B-34B parameter models)
- Storage: 100 GB NVMe SSD
- GPU: Optional but delivers 10-50x inference speedup

Cost Comparison

Private RAG with RuVector (one-time hardware cost + electricity):
- Embedding: $0 (local ONNX model)
- Storage: $0 (local files)
- LLM inference: $0 (local Ollama)
- Compliance overhead: Low (data stays on-premise)

Versus cloud RAG:
- OpenAI embeddings: $0.02 per 1M tokens
- Pinecone: $70+/month for 1M vectors
- GPT-4 API: $30 per 1M output tokens
- Compliance overhead: High (data leaves premises, DPA required)

title: Building a Fully Private RAG Stack: Step-by-Step Setup Guide
Private RAG Document Ingestion Pipeline: Patterns and Best Practices
WASM Model Deployment Patterns: Browser vs Server vs Container
content:
Deploying AI models as WASM modules gives you three distinct deployment targets, each with different trade-offs. Understanding which to use is essential for private AI architecture.

Deployment Target Comparison

| Dimension | Browser WASM | Server WASM (Node.js) | Container (Docker + Ollama) |
|-----------|-------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Data location | Client device | Server (yours) | Server (yours) |
| Model size limit | ~2-4 GB | No practical limit | No practical limit |
| First-load time | 1-30 sec (model download) | Milliseconds | 1-5 sec startup |
| GPU access | WebGPU (experimental) | Full CUDA/Metal | Full CUDA/Metal |
| Throughput | Low (browser JS thread) | Medium | High |
| Privacy | Maximum (data never sent) | High (controlled server) | High (controlled server) |
| Offline capability | Yes (after cache) | Requires server | Requires server |

Browser WASM Deployment

Best for: Healthcare apps where patient data must not leave the device, legal document review, personal finance analysis.

RuVector's browser WASM runtime is 5.5 KB. The model itself is the large download:

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<script type="module">
import init, { VectorDB } from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@ruvector/ruvector-wasm';

// Vector search runs 100% in browser
await init();
const db = new VectorDB({ dimensions: 384 });

// wllama: run Llama models in browser via WASM
import { Wllama } from '@wllama/wllama';
const wllama = new Wllama('/wllama-worker.js');

// Download model once, cache in IndexedDB
await wllama.loadModelFromUrl(
  'https://huggingface.co/models/Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct-q4.gguf',
  { n_ctx: 4096 }
);

// From here: all inference is local, no network calls
const answer = await wllama.createCompletion(prompt);
</script>
</html>
```

Server WASM / Node.js Deployment

Best for: API services handling confidential documents, multi-user private RAG, enterprise search.

RuVector uses napi-rs for native Node.js bindings (not WASM in this context, but the same Rust code). The ONNX embedding layer in this codebase uses WASM SIMD:

```javascript
// This pattern is used in src/storage/kb-embed.js
const svc = await createEmbeddingServiceAsync({
  provider: 'transformers',     // Uses ONNX Runtime Web (WASM backend)
  model: 'Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2',
  dimensions: 384
});
// Model loaded once, runs inference in WASM SIMD
// ~930 embeds/sec on Apple M3 Max
```

Containerized Local LLM (Docker + Ollama)

Best for: Team-shared private AI, standardised deployment, infrastructure-as-code.

```yaml
# docker-compose.yml for private RAG stack
version: '3.8'
services:
  ollama:
    image: ollama/ollama:latest
    ports:
      - "11434:11434"
    volumes:
      - ollama-data:/root/.ollama
    # GPU support (optional)
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: 1
              capabilities: [gpu]
  
  ruvector:
    image: ruvnet/ruvector-postgres:latest
    ports:
      - "5435:5432"
    volumes:
      - ruvector-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${RUVECTOR_PASSWORD}
  
  app:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    environment:
      RUVECTOR_HOST: ruvector
      RUVECTOR_PORT: 5432
      OLLAMA_HOST: http://ollama:11434
    depends_on:
      - ollama
      - ruvector

volumes:
  ollama-data:
  ruvector-data:
```

```bash
# Start the entire private AI stack
docker compose up -d

# Pull model inside container
docker exec ollama ollama pull llama3.1:8b

# Verify no external calls once running
docker exec app curl -s http://ollama:11434/api/tags
```

RVF Cognitive Container (RuVector-native deployment)

RuVector's RVF format packages everything into one file:

```bash
# Create a self-contained .rvf file with vectors + model + WASM runtime
rvf-cli pack \
  --vectors ./my-vectors.bin \
  --model ./llama3.gguf \
  --wasm-runtime embedded \
  --output my-private-ai.rvf

# Deploy: copy single file to any machine
scp my-private-ai.rvf prod-server:/opt/ai/

# Boot as microservice (125ms startup)
rvf-cli run my-private-ai.rvf --port 8080
```

This boots a full Linux microVM with the AI stack inside. Network is isolated by default.

Selecting the Right Pattern

- Maximum privacy (data never transmitted): Browser WASM
- Team sharing + compliance logging: Container (Docker + Ollama)
- Single-file simplicity: RVF cognitive container
- Highest throughput: Native server with GPU (CUDA/Metal)

title: On-Premises and Air-Gapped AI Deployment with RuVector
content:
Air-gapped deployment means no internet connectivity at runtime. This is required for classified government systems, financial trading environments, healthcare systems subject to strict data residency, and industrial control systems.

What Must Be Downloaded Before Air-Gapping

Before disconnecting from the internet, acquire:

1. Model files (GGUF format):
```bash
# Download via Ollama (stores in ~/.ollama/models)
ollama pull phi3:mini          # 2.3 GB
ollama pull llama3.1:8b        # 4.9 GB
ollama pull mistral:7b-instruct # 4.1 GB

# Or download directly from HuggingFace
wget https://huggingface.co/microsoft/Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct-gguf/resolve/main/Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct-q4.gguf
```

2. Embedding model:
```bash
# Pre-download the ONNX embedding model cache
node -e "
  const { createEmbeddingServiceAsync } = require('@claude-flow/embeddings');
  // This downloads and caches the model
  const svc = await createEmbeddingServiceAsync({
    provider: 'transformers',
    model: 'Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2'
  });
  await svc.embed('warmup');
  console.log('Model cached at:', process.env.HOME + '/.cache/huggingface');
"
```

3. Docker images (if using containers):
```bash
docker pull ollama/ollama:latest
docker pull ruvnet/ruvector-postgres:latest
docker save ollama/ollama:latest | gzip > ollama.tar.gz
docker save ruvnet/ruvector-postgres:latest | gzip > ruvector.tar.gz
# Transfer to air-gapped environment
docker load < ollama.tar.gz
```

4. npm packages:
```bash
# Pack the entire node_modules
npm pack
# Or use a private npm mirror (Verdaccio)
npx verdaccio &
npm set registry http://localhost:4873
```

Air-Gapped Network Configuration

```bash
# On the air-gapped server, block all outbound connections
# but allow internal services
iptables -P OUTPUT DROP
iptables -A OUTPUT -d 127.0.0.0/8 -j ACCEPT          # localhost
iptables -A OUTPUT -d 192.168.0.0/16 -j ACCEPT       # LAN only
iptables -A OUTPUT -d 10.0.0.0/8 -j ACCEPT           # Private network

# Verify Ollama works without internet
curl http://localhost:11434/api/generate -d '{
  "model": "llama3.1:8b",
  "prompt": "Hello",
  "stream": false
}'
```

Environment Variables for Offline Mode

```bash
# Prevent @xenova/transformers from attempting downloads
export TRANSFORMERS_OFFLINE=1
export HF_DATASETS_OFFLINE=1

# Point to local model cache
export TRANSFORMERS_CACHE=/opt/models/huggingface
export HF_HOME=/opt/models/huggingface

# RuVector configuration (local only)
export RUVECTOR_HOST=localhost
export RUVECTOR_PORT=5435

# Disable telemetry
export DO_NOT_TRACK=1
export HUGGINGFACE_HUB_VERBOSITY=error
```

Verifying Air-Gap Compliance

```bash
# Run the full RAG query and monitor network
strace -e trace=network node your-rag-app.js 2>&1 | grep -v '127.0\|::1'
# Should produce no output (no external network calls)

# Or use tcpdump
tcpdump -i any -n not host 127.0.0.1 and not host 192.168.0.0/16
# Should show nothing during RAG queries
```

Data Residency Compliance Checklist

- [ ] All model files stored on premises
- [ ] No cloud API keys in the application
- [ ] ONNX embedding model cached locally (TRANSFORMERS_OFFLINE=1)
- [ ] Ollama configured to not call HuggingFace at runtime
- [ ] RuVector database on local PostgreSQL or file system
- [ ] WAL and backup files stay on-premises
- [ ] Audit logs stored locally (not shipped to cloud logging)
- [ ] Docker images loaded from local registry or tar files
- [ ] npm packages installed from local mirror or vendored node_modules

RuVector's Tamper-Evident Audit Trail

For regulated industries, RuVector's RVF format provides cryptographic audit trails:

```bash
# Every insert, query, and delete is hash-linked
# Witness chain uses Ed25519 signatures
rvf-cli audit-log my-private-ai.rvf --from 2026-01-01 --to 2026-12-31
# Produces: who queried what, when, with cryptographic proof
# Required for HIPAA, financial audit trails
```

title: Model Selection Guide for Private CPU-Only Deployment
content:
Selecting the right model for private deployment is a balance between quality, speed, and hardware constraints. This guide covers models that work well without a GPU.

CPU-Only Model Tiers

Tier 1 - Fast (suitable for real-time chat, sub-5-second responses on modern CPU):

Phi-3-mini (3.8B parameters, Microsoft):
- GGUF Q4 size: 2.3 GB RAM
- CPU tokens/sec: 15-30 t/s on M3 Max, 8-15 t/s on Intel i7
- Strengths: Coding, reasoning, instruction following
- Context: 4K or 128K depending on variant
- Best for: Enterprise chat assistants, code review, document Q&A
- Install: `ollama pull phi3:mini`

Llama 3.2 3B (Meta):
- GGUF Q4 size: 2.0 GB RAM
- CPU tokens/sec: 20-35 t/s on M3 Max
- Strengths: Multilingual, general purpose
- Context: 128K
- Best for: Multi-language document processing
- Install: `ollama pull llama3.2:3b`

Gemma 2 2B (Google):
- GGUF Q4 size: 1.6 GB RAM
- CPU tokens/sec: 25-45 t/s on M3 Max
- Strengths: Very fast, efficient architecture
- Context: 8K
- Best for: Classification, summarization, edge deployment
- Install: `ollama pull gemma2:2b`

Tier 2 - Balanced (5-15 second responses, recommended for most private RAG):

Llama 3.1 8B (Meta):
- GGUF Q4 size: 4.9 GB RAM
- CPU tokens/sec: 8-18 t/s on M3 Max, 3-7 t/s on Intel i7
- Strengths: Strong reasoning, code, broad knowledge
- Context: 128K
- Best for: General enterprise RAG, customer support, research
- Install: `ollama pull llama3.1:8b`

Mistral 7B Instruct (Mistral AI):
- GGUF Q4 size: 4.1 GB RAM
- CPU tokens/sec: 9-20 t/s on M3 Max
- Strengths: European regulatory knowledge, multilingual
- Context: 32K
- Best for: GDPR-sensitive workloads, European enterprise
- Install: `ollama pull mistral:7b-instruct`

Qwen2.5 7B (Alibaba):
- GGUF Q4 size: 4.7 GB RAM
- Strengths: Code, math, structured output
- Context: 128K
- Install: `ollama pull qwen2.5:7b`

Tier 3 - High Quality (15-60+ second responses, for batch processing):

Llama 3.1 70B (Meta):
- GGUF Q4 size: 40 GB RAM required
- CPU tokens/sec: 1-4 t/s
- Strengths: Near-GPT-4 quality for complex reasoning
- Best for: Overnight batch analysis, legal document review
- Requires 64 GB+ RAM machine

Quantization Trade-offs

| Quantization | Size vs FP16 | Quality vs FP16 | Speed vs FP16 |
|-------------|-------------|----------------|---------------|
| Q8_0 | 50% | 99.8% | 2x |
| Q5_K_M | 35% | 99.5% | 2.8x |
| Q4_K_M | 28% | 99.0% | 3.5x (recommended default) |
| Q3_K_M | 22% | 97.5% | 4x |
| Q2_K | 15% | 92% | 5x (avoid for RAG) |

Recommendation: Use Q4_K_M for the best quality/speed/size balance. The 1% quality loss is imperceptible in RAG contexts where retrieval quality matters more than generation precision.

Models That Fit in Browser WASM (Under 4 GB)

For browser deployment with wllama:
- Phi-3-mini Q4: 2.3 GB (works in Chrome/Firefox)
- Llama 3.2 3B Q4: 2.0 GB (works in all modern browsers)
- Gemma 2 2B Q4: 1.6 GB (fastest browser inference)
- TinyLlama 1.1B Q4: 0.7 GB (minimal footprint for edge)

Browser download + inference requires:
- Cross-Origin headers: Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp
- SharedArrayBuffer enabled (needed for wllama multi-threading)
- Progressive download with IndexedDB caching for repeat users

RuvLTRA Models (RuVector Pre-trained)

RuVector provides specialized GGUF models optimised for routing and embeddings:

```bash
# Install from HuggingFace
wget https://huggingface.co/ruv/ruvltra/resolve/main/ruvltra-routing-q4.gguf

# Use with ruvllm
npm install @ruvector/ruvllm
```

RuvLTRA delivers <10ms inference for routing decisions, making it suitable for real-time agent routing in Ruflo V3.

title: Security Architecture for Private AI: Network Isolation and Zero-Trust
content:
Building a private AI system requires deliberate security architecture. This covers network isolation, data containment, and the layered defence approach used in production private AI deployments.

Threat Model for Private AI

The primary threats in private AI deployments are:
1. Data exfiltration via model API calls (embedding or LLM endpoints call home)
2. Model poisoning via compromised weights downloaded over unsecured channels
3. Query exposure via logging to cloud observability platforms
4. Side-channel leakage via model output (training data memorisation)
5. Prompt injection in RAG context (attacker-controlled documents trigger exfiltration)

Network Isolation Architecture

Production private AI network topology:

```
[Internet] <--blocked--> [Firewall]
                              |
                    [Internal Network]
                    /                \
         [AI Services Zone]    [Data Zone]
         - Ollama :11434       - RuVector DB :5435
         - App Server :3000    - Document Store
         - Vector API          - Audit Logs
                    \
             [User Access Zone]
             - HTTPS Proxy :443
             - VPN Gateway
```

Firewall Rules (Linux iptables)

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# private-ai-firewall.sh

# Reset rules
iptables -F
iptables -X

# Default policies: drop everything
iptables -P INPUT DROP
iptables -P OUTPUT DROP
iptables -P FORWARD DROP

# Allow established connections
iptables -A INPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
iptables -A OUTPUT -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

# Allow loopback
iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
iptables -A OUTPUT -o lo -j ACCEPT

# Allow internal services only
iptables -A OUTPUT -d 10.0.0.0/8 -j ACCEPT     # Internal LAN
iptables -A OUTPUT -d 172.16.0.0/12 -j ACCEPT  # Docker networks

# Block all external internet from AI services
# AI services run as user 'aiuser' (UID 1001)
iptables -A OUTPUT -m owner --uid-owner 1001 -j DROP

# Allow users to reach the app (via internal reverse proxy only)
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 3000 -s 10.0.0.0/8 -j ACCEPT

echo "Private AI firewall rules applied"
```

DNS-Based Exfiltration Prevention

Even with IP blocking, DNS queries can leak data:

```bash
# Use local DNS resolver, block all upstream except internal
# /etc/resolv.conf
nameserver 10.0.0.1      # Internal DNS only
search internal.company.com

# Block DNS to external resolvers
iptables -A OUTPUT -p udp --dport 53 -d 1.1.1.1 -j DROP
iptables -A OUTPUT -p udp --dport 53 -d 8.8.8.8 -j DROP
```

Secret Management (No API Keys in Code)

```bash
# Use environment variables from a secrets manager, not .env files
# HashiCorp Vault example:
vault kv put secret/ai-stack \
  ruvector_password=$(openssl rand -hex 32)

# Inject at runtime
export RUVECTOR_PASSWORD=$(vault kv get -field=ruvector_password secret/ai-stack)

# Never in code:
# BAD: const password = 'hardcoded123'
# BAD: RUVECTOR_PASSWORD=mypassword in .env committed to git
# GOOD: process.env.RUVECTOR_PASSWORD (injected from vault at startup)
```

AIDefence Integration (Ruflo V3)

Ruflo V3 includes AIDefence threat detection for private AI deployments:

- Prompt injection detection (<10ms latency)
- Sensitive data pattern scanning (PII, credit cards, SSNs)
- Anomaly detection on query patterns
- Rate limiting per user/session

```javascript
// The ruvnet/ruflo AIDefence layer
const { AIDefence } = require('@claude-flow/security');

const defence = new AIDefence({
  promptInjectionThreshold: 0.8,
  piiPatterns: ['ssn', 'credit-card', 'email'],
  auditLog: './audit/ai-queries.log'
});

// In your RAG endpoint:
app.post('/query', async (req, res) => {
  const threat = await defence.scan(req.body.query);
  if (threat.score > 0.8) {
    return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Query blocked' });
  }
  // Proceed with RAG...
});
```

Post-Quantum Signatures for Model Integrity

RuVector's RVF format supports post-quantum signatures:
- ML-DSA-65 (CRYSTALS-Dilithium) for post-quantum model signing
- Ed25519 for current deployment (faster verification)
- SLH-DSA-128s for maximum security

```bash
# Sign your model file before distribution
rvf-cli sign my-private-ai.rvf --algorithm ml-dsa-65 --key signing.key

# Verify on deployment host
rvf-cli verify my-private-ai.rvf --public-key signing.pub
# Ensures model was not tampered with during transfer
```

This is essential for supply chain security: verifying that the model weights you deploy are exactly what was signed by the originating team.

title: GDPR and HIPAA Compliance with Local AI: What Private Deployment Actually Achieves
content:
Private AI deployment significantly reduces compliance burden, but it does not automatically make you compliant. Understanding what private deployment achieves and what additional controls are needed.

What Private AI Deployment Actually Provides

1. Data minimisation (GDPR Article 5)
   - Data is not transmitted to third-party processors
   - No data residency issues (data stays in your jurisdiction)
   - Eliminates the need for data processing agreements with AI vendors

2. Purpose limitation (GDPR Article 5)
   - You control exactly what data enters the AI system
   - No risk of your data being used for third-party model training

3. HIPAA Safe Harbor
   - Protected Health Information (PHI) never leaves your HIPAA-covered environment
   - No Business Associate Agreement needed with an AI vendor
   - Your existing HIPAA infrastructure controls apply

What Private Deployment Does NOT Automatically Provide

- Access controls: You must implement authentication and authorization
- Audit trails: You must log who queried what
- Data subject rights: You must still respond to erasure requests (delete from vector DB)
- Model output risk: LLMs can memorise and reproduce training data (a separate concern)
- Breach notification: Still required if your on-premises system is compromised

RuVector Audit Trail for HIPAA/GDPR

```javascript
// Audit every query in a structured log
const { createWriteStream } = require('fs');
const auditLog = createWriteStream('./audit/queries.jsonl', { flags: 'a' });

async function auditedRagQuery(userId, question, db, embedder) {
  const queryId = crypto.randomUUID();
  const startTime = Date.now();
  
  // Log query start (do NOT log the actual PHI question in some HIPAA contexts)
  auditLog.write(JSON.stringify({
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
    queryId,
    userId,
    action: 'rag_query_start',
    questionHash: crypto.createHash('sha256').update(question).digest('hex')
  }) + '\n');
  
  const results = await ragQuery(db, embedder, question);
  
  auditLog.write(JSON.stringify({
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
    queryId,
    action: 'rag_query_complete',
    resultsCount: results.length,
    latencyMs: Date.now() - startTime,
    sourcesAccessed: results.map(r => r.metadata.source)
  }) + '\n');
  
  return results;
}
```

Data Subject Erasure (GDPR Right to Be Forgotten)

When a data subject requests deletion, you must delete their data from the vector database:

```javascript
// Delete all vectors associated with a user's documents
async function eraseUserData(db, userId) {
  // Search for all entries from this user
  const allEntries = db.metadata;
  const toDelete = [];
  
  for (const [id, meta] of allEntries) {
    if (meta.uploadedBy === userId || meta.dataSubjectId === userId) {
      toDelete.push(id);
    }
  }
  
  for (const id of toDelete) {
    await db.delete(id);
  }
  
  await db.flush(); // Persist deletion
  
  // Log the erasure
  auditLog.write(JSON.stringify({
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
    action: 'data_erasure',
    userId,
    deletedVectors: toDelete.length,
    gdprArticle: '17'
  }) + '\n');
  
  return toDelete.length;
}
```

Encryption at Rest

RuVector's binary format stores vectors unencrypted by default. For regulated industries, add filesystem-level encryption:

```bash
# Linux: LUKS encryption for the vector store volume
cryptsetup luksFormat /dev/sdb
cryptsetup open /dev/sdb ruvector-encrypted
mkfs.ext4 /dev/mapper/ruvector-encrypted
mount /dev/mapper/ruvector-encrypted /var/ruvector

# macOS: FileVault (system-level) or encrypted APFS volume
diskutil apfs addVolume disk1 APFSX "ruvector-encrypted" -passphrase
```

Encryption in Transit (Internal Services)

Even within your private network, encrypt service-to-service communication:

```bash
# Generate internal TLS certificates
openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 3650 \
  -newkey rsa:4096 \
  -keyout /etc/ssl/private/ruvector.key \
  -out /etc/ssl/certs/ruvector.crt \
  -subj '/CN=ruvector.internal'

# Configure PostgreSQL ruvector to use TLS
# postgresql.conf:
# ssl = on
# ssl_cert_file = '/etc/ssl/certs/ruvector.crt'
# ssl_key_file = '/etc/ssl/private/ruvector.key'
```

Compliance Checklist Summary

GDPR:
- [ ] Data stays in EU jurisdiction (or contractually permitted jurisdiction)
- [ ] Audit log for all data accesses
- [ ] Erasure mechanism implemented and tested
- [ ] DPA required only for internal processors (employees), not third-party AI vendors
- [ ] Privacy impact assessment (DPIA) documented

HIPAA:
- [ ] PHI never transmitted to external systems
- [ ] Access control with user authentication
- [ ] Audit controls (who accessed what PHI)
- [ ] Transmission security (TLS for internal services)
- [ ] Media disposal (secure deletion of model files containing PHI)
- [ ] Business continuity for the AI system

ISO 27001 (AI systems annex A.11):
- [ ] Model provenance documented (where did weights come from)
- [ ] Change management for model updates
- [ ] Incident response for AI-specific events

title: Integrating Ruflo V3 with Ollama as Local LLM Backend
content:
Ruflo V3 normally orchestrates agents using Anthropic's Claude models. For fully private deployment, you can configure it to use Ollama (local LLMs) as the provider.

Provider Architecture in Ruflo V3

Ruflo V3 uses a multi-provider system. The provider registry supports:
- claude (Anthropic, cloud)
- openai (OpenAI, cloud)
- gemini (Google, cloud)
- ollama (local, private)
- groq (cloud, fast inference)

For private deployment, route all traffic through ollama.

Configuring Ollama as Provider

```bash
# Install Ollama and pull a capable model
ollama pull llama3.1:8b

# Verify Ollama is running
curl http://localhost:11434/api/tags
```

```javascript
// ruflo.config.json for private deployment
{
  "providers": {
    "default": "ollama",
    "ollama": {
      "baseUrl": "http://localhost:11434",
      "model": "llama3.1:8b",
      "options": {
        "temperature": 0.7,
        "num_ctx": 8192,
        "num_predict": 2048
      }
    }
  },
  "memory": {
    "backend": "hybrid",
    "path": "./data/memory"
  },
  "agents": {
    "maxConcurrent": 4,
    "timeout": 120000
  }
}
```

```bash
# Initialize Ruflo with local Ollama provider
npx ruflo@latest config set provider ollama
npx ruflo@latest config set ollama.baseUrl http://localhost:11434
npx ruflo@latest config set ollama.model llama3.1:8b

# Verify provider is working
npx ruflo@latest providers test --provider ollama
```

Spawning Agents with Local LLM

```bash
# Initialize a private swarm
npx ruflo@latest swarm init \
  --topology hierarchical \
  --max-agents 4 \
  --strategy specialized

# Spawn agents that will use Ollama internally
npx ruflo@latest agent spawn -t researcher --name local-researcher
npx ruflo@latest agent spawn -t coder --name local-coder
```

Provider-Level Routing for Hybrid Deployments

For a hybrid where simple tasks use local LLM but complex tasks use Claude:

```javascript
// Provider routing configuration (ruflo.config.json)
{
  "providers": {
    "routing": {
      "strategy": "task-complexity",
      "rules": [
        {
          "condition": { "complexity": "low", "private": true },
          "provider": "ollama",
          "model": "phi3:mini"
        },
        {
          "condition": { "complexity": "medium" },
          "provider": "ollama",
          "model": "llama3.1:8b"
        },
        {
          "condition": { "complexity": "high", "allowCloud": true },
          "provider": "claude",
          "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6"
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

Self-Hosted Memory (No Cloud Memory Calls)

Ruflo V3's memory system uses RuVector locally:

```bash
# Initialize local memory (uses local HNSW, no cloud)
npx ruflo@latest memory init --backend local

# Store patterns locally
npx ruflo@latest memory store \
  --key "private-pattern-1" \
  --value "Always use local embedding model" \
  --namespace private-patterns

# Search (uses local HNSW, 150x-12,500x faster than brute force)
npx ruflo@latest memory search \
  --query "embedding model configuration" \
  --namespace private-patterns
```

Performance Expectations with Ollama

Compared to Claude API, local Ollama will be slower for large context:
- Claude Sonnet API: ~80 tokens/sec output
- Llama 3.1 8B Q4 on M3 Max (Metal): 40-80 tokens/sec
- Llama 3.1 8B Q4 on CPU only (M3 Max): 8-18 tokens/sec
- Llama 3.1 8B Q4 on Intel i7 CPU: 3-7 tokens/sec

For agentic workflows with many small tasks (routing, classification, short summaries), use phi3:mini which is 2-3x faster.

Monitoring Local Inference

```bash
# Watch Ollama resource usage
ollama ps
# Shows: model loaded, RAM used, GPU layers

# Ollama API for metrics
curl http://localhost:11434/api/metrics

# System resource monitoring during agent swarm
npx ruflo@latest hooks statusline --json
# Shows: CPU%, memory, active agents
```

title: Performance Optimization for Local WASM LLM: Token Speed and Context Management
RuVector HNSW Index: Understanding the 125x Search Speedup
content:
The Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) index is the core data structure that gives RuVector its 61 microsecond p50 search latency. Understanding how it works helps you configure it correctly.

What HNSW Is

HNSW is an approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search algorithm that builds a multi-layer graph where:
- Layer 0 contains all vectors with short-range connections
- Higher layers contain increasingly sparse subsets with long-range connections
- Search starts at the top layer (few nodes, coarse navigation) and descends

This is why it is called hierarchical: the layering creates a skip-list-like structure that lets you jump quickly to the right neighbourhood before doing fine-grained comparison.

Why 125x Faster Than SQLite Brute Force

Brute-force search in SQLite (as used in earlier versions of this project) requires comparing the query vector against every stored vector:
- 17,000 vectors x 384 dimensions x 2 multiplications = 13 million float operations
- SQLite overhead: rows are fetched, cast to Float32Array, then compared one-by-one
- Typical result: 7.5ms per search at 17K vectors

HNSW search:
- Navigates approximately log(n) nodes at each layer
- For 17,000 vectors: visits approximately 40-80 vectors total (not all 17,000)
- Result: 61 microseconds p50 (from RuVector benchmarks)
- Ratio: 7.5ms / 0.061ms = 123x faster (rounded to "125x" in documentation)

HNSW Configuration Parameters

```javascript
const db = await getPersistentVectorDB('kb', {
  dimensions: 384,
  distanceMetric: 'Cosine',
  // HNSW parameters (if exposed by ruvector version you use):
  hnsw: {
    M: 16,           // Number of bidirectional links per node
                     // Higher = better recall, more RAM, slower insert
                     // Default 16 is good for most cases
                     // Use 32 for maximum recall
    efConstruction: 200, // Size of dynamic candidate list during build
                         // Higher = better index quality, slower insert
                         // 200 is a good default
    efSearch: 50,    // Size of dynamic candidate list during search
                     // Higher = better recall, slower search
                     // 50 gives ~99% recall for most workloads
  }
});
```

Recall vs Speed Trade-off

| efSearch | Recall@10 | Search latency |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| 10 | ~95% | 30µs |
| 50 | ~99% | 61µs (default) |
| 100 | ~99.5% | 110µs |
| 500 | ~99.9% | 450µs |

For RAG use cases, 99% recall is sufficient and often imperceptible from 99.9%.

GNN Layer on Top of HNSW

RuVector adds a Graph Neural Network layer on top of HNSW:

```
Query -> HNSW Index -> Top-K candidates -> GNN layer -> Reranked results
                                                 ^
                                        Learns from feedback
```

The GNN applies multi-head attention to the candidate set, reweighting based on:
- Graph topology (which documents link to which)
- Historical query patterns (what users clicked on for similar queries)
- Freshness signals

Result: +12.4% recall improvement over vanilla HNSW after training.

HNSW vs Brute-Force Fallback in PersistentVectorDB

The PersistentVectorDB in this codebase (src/storage/persistent-vector-db.js) uses RuVector's HNSW when the ruvector package is available. It falls back to brute-force cosine similarity when ruvector is not loaded:

```javascript
// From persistent-vector-db.js: fallback search implementation
fallbackSearch(query) {
  const results = [];
  for (const [id, vector] of this.vectorCache.entries()) {
    const score = this.cosineSimilarity(queryVec, vector);
    results.push({ id, score, metadata: this.metadata.get(id) });
  }
  return results.sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score).slice(0, k);
}
```

The fallback is functionally correct but O(n) rather than O(log n). At 1,000 vectors the difference is imperceptible; at 100,000 vectors it becomes a bottleneck.

Scaling Limits

- RuVector HNSW with PQ8 compression: 1M vectors in ~200 MB RAM
- Search latency grows logarithmically: 61µs at 17K vectors, ~120µs at 1M vectors
- Build time: approximately 1-3ms per vector insert at M=16
- Rebuild not required: HNSW is an online index (incremental inserts)

PostgreSQL Extension Mode (ruvector-postgres)

This project connects to ruvector-postgres on port 5435, which runs the same HNSW algorithm as a PostgreSQL extension:

```sql
-- Create a vector column
CREATE TABLE documents (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  content TEXT,
  embedding ruvector(384)
);

-- Create HNSW index
CREATE INDEX ON documents USING hnsw (embedding ruvector_cosine_ops)
  WITH (m = 16, ef_construction = 200);

-- Search (uses HNSW automatically)
SELECT id, content
FROM documents
ORDER BY embedding <=> ruvector_embed('my query text')
LIMIT 10;
```

title: Self-Hosted Agent Infrastructure with Ruflo V3: Private Enterprise Deployment
content:
Running Ruflo V3 agent swarms entirely on your own infrastructure requires configuring each component for private operation. This covers the full enterprise self-hosted stack.

Infrastructure Components

A self-hosted Ruflo V3 private deployment requires:

1. Agent orchestrator: Ruflo V3 CLI (runs locally, no cloud dependency for orchestration)
2. LLM backend: Ollama on your servers
3. Memory: Local RuVector file DB or ruvector-postgres
4. MCP servers: Run on localhost (no cloud MCP proxies)
5. Hooks and learning: Local agentdb.db (SQLite + HNSW)

In this project, agentdb.db is the local SQLite database for agent state and learning patterns. It is in the project root:
```
/Users/stuartkerr/Code/Ask-Ruvnet/Ask-Ruvnet/agentdb.db
```

Enterprise Self-Hosted Architecture

```yaml
# docker-compose.enterprise.yml
version: '3.9'

services:
  # LLM inference (private)
  ollama:
    image: ollama/ollama:latest
    ports: ["11434:11434"]
    volumes: ["ollama_models:/root/.ollama"]
    environment:
      OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE: 60m
      OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL: 4
    # Uncomment for GPU:
    # runtime: nvidia
    
  # Vector database (private)
  ruvector-postgres:
    image: ruvnet/ruvector-postgres:latest
    ports: ["5435:5432"]
    volumes: ["ruvector_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data"]
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: postgres
      POSTGRES_USER: postgres
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD_FILE: /run/secrets/ruvector_password
    secrets: [ruvector_password]
    
  # Agent application
  agent-app:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
    ports: ["3000:3000"]
    volumes:
      - ./data:/app/data          # Local vector files
      - ./agentdb.db:/app/agentdb.db  # Agent learning state
      - ./memory:/app/memory      # Ruflo memory
    environment:
      RUVECTOR_HOST: ruvector-postgres
      RUVECTOR_PORT: 5432
      OLLAMA_HOST: http://ollama:11434
      ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ""       # Empty = no cloud API
      CLAUDE_FLOW_MEMORY_BACKEND: hybrid
    depends_on: [ollama, ruvector-postgres]
    networks: [private-ai]

networks:
  private-ai:
    driver: bridge
    internal: true  # No external connectivity

secrets:
  ruvector_password:
    file: ./secrets/ruvector_password.txt
    
volumes:
  ollama_models:
  ruvector_data:
```

Model Pre-loading Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# preload-models.sh - Run once after docker compose up

# Wait for Ollama to be ready
until curl -s http://localhost:11434/api/tags > /dev/null; do
  echo "Waiting for Ollama..."
  sleep 2
done

# Pull models for different agent roles
ollama pull phi3:mini        # Fast routing, classification (coordinator agent)
ollama pull llama3.1:8b     # Research, coding (complex tasks)
ollama pull mistral:7b-instruct  # Document analysis

echo "Models ready. Enterprise private AI stack operational."
```

Ruflo Agent Configuration for Private Operation

```javascript
// ruflo.config.json
{
  "providers": {
    "default": "ollama",
    "ollama": {
      "baseUrl": "http://ollama:11434",
      "models": {
        "fast": "phi3:mini",
        "balanced": "llama3.1:8b",
        "quality": "mistral:7b-instruct"
      }
    }
  },
  "memory": {
    "backend": "hybrid",
    "path": "/app/memory",
    "vectorDB": {
      "type": "ruvector",
      "host": "ruvector-postgres",
      "port": 5432
    }
  },
  "security": {
    "aiDefence": true,
    "promptInjection": "block",
    "auditLog": "/app/logs/audit.jsonl"
  },
  "swarm": {
    "topology": "hierarchical",
    "maxAgents": 8,
    "consensus": "raft"
  }
}
```

Agent Role to Model Mapping

For cost/speed optimisation in private deployment:

```javascript
// Assign smaller models to routine agents, larger to complex ones
const agentModelMap = {
  'coordinator': 'phi3:mini',    // Routing decisions, fast
  'researcher': 'llama3.1:8b',  // Complex document analysis
  'coder': 'llama3.1:8b',       // Code generation
  'reviewer': 'mistral:7b-instruct', // Code review, reasoning
  'tester': 'phi3:mini',        // Test generation, simpler
  'summarizer': 'phi3:mini'     // Summarization, fast
};
```

Monitoring the Private Stack

```bash
# Ruflo status
npx ruflo@latest status --watch

# Ollama inference stats
curl http://localhost:11434/api/ps

# RuVector DB stats
psql -h localhost -p 5435 -U postgres -c \
  "SELECT COUNT(*) as vectors, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size('openclaw_memory.operational_knowledge')) as size FROM openclaw_memory.operational_knowledge;"

# Agent memory usage
npx ruflo@latest memory list --namespace patterns --limit 20
```

title: RuVector Semantic Search Pipeline: Query Expansion, Reranking, and Context Compression
RuVector PostgreSQL Extension: Private Vector DB Setup and SQL Interface
content:
RuVector ships as a PostgreSQL extension that replaces pgvector with superior performance. This is the preferred storage backend for production private RAG deployments (as used in this project on port 5435).

Installation

```bash
# Option 1: Docker (recommended for private deployment)
docker pull ruvnet/ruvector-postgres:latest

docker run -d \
  --name ruvector-db \
  -p 5435:5432 \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=yourpassword \
  -v ruvector-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
  ruvnet/ruvector-postgres:latest

# Option 2: Build from source (air-gapped environments)
git clone https://github.com/ruvnet/ruvector
cd ruvector
cargo build --release --features postgres
# Follow PostgreSQL extension install instructions
```

Database Schema for Private RAG

```sql
-- Connect to your private RuVector PostgreSQL
-- psql -h localhost -p 5435 -U postgres

-- Create schema for your application
CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS private_rag;

-- Enable RuVector extension
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS ruvector;

-- Main documents table
CREATE TABLE private_rag.documents (
  id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  title       TEXT NOT NULL,
  content     TEXT NOT NULL,
  source      TEXT,                    -- File path or URL (internal only)
  department  TEXT,                    -- For access control
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  embedding   ruvector(384)            -- 384-dim vector
);

-- HNSW index (125x faster than brute-force)
CREATE INDEX ON private_rag.documents
  USING hnsw (embedding ruvector_cosine_ops)
  WITH (m = 16, ef_construction = 200);

-- Full-text search index (for hybrid search)
CREATE INDEX ON private_rag.documents
  USING gin (to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || content));
```

Inserting Documents with Local Embeddings

```sql
-- Option 1: Let ruvector_embed() generate embedding (uses local model)
INSERT INTO private_rag.documents (title, content, source, department)
VALUES (
  'GDPR Privacy Policy',
  'This policy governs how we handle personal data...',
  '/docs/legal/gdpr-policy.pdf',
  'legal'
);
-- The embedding column is automatically populated by ruvector_embed() trigger
-- OR pass it explicitly:

-- Option 2: Pre-compute embedding externally (ONNX) and insert
INSERT INTO private_rag.documents (title, content, source, embedding)
VALUES (
  'Security Architecture Guide',
  'Zero-trust network principles require...',
  '/docs/security/arch.md',
  '[0.123, -0.456, 0.789, ...]'::ruvector(384)
);
```

Semantic Search Queries

```sql
-- Basic semantic search
SELECT
  id,
  title,
  content,
  1 - (embedding <=> ruvector_embed('How do I configure HNSW?')) AS similarity
FROM private_rag.documents
ORDER BY embedding <=> ruvector_embed('How do I configure HNSW?')
LIMIT 10;

-- Hybrid search (vector + full-text, weighted)
WITH vector_results AS (
  SELECT id, 1 - (embedding <=> ruvector_embed($1)) AS vscore
  FROM private_rag.documents
  ORDER BY embedding <=> ruvector_embed($1)
  LIMIT 20
),
text_results AS (
  SELECT id, ts_rank(to_tsvector('english', content), query) AS tscore
  FROM private_rag.documents,
       plainto_tsquery('english', $1) AS query
  WHERE to_tsvector('english', content) @@ query
  LIMIT 20
)
SELECT
  d.id, d.title, d.content,
  COALESCE(v.vscore * 0.7, 0) + COALESCE(t.tscore * 0.3, 0) AS hybrid_score
FROM private_rag.documents d
LEFT JOIN vector_results v ON d.id = v.id
LEFT JOIN text_results t ON d.id = t.id
WHERE v.id IS NOT NULL OR t.id IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY hybrid_score DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Filtered search (department-based access control)
SELECT id, title, content
FROM private_rag.documents
WHERE department = 'legal'
  AND embedding <=> ruvector_embed('data retention policy') < 0.3  -- threshold
ORDER BY embedding <=> ruvector_embed('data retention policy')
LIMIT 5;
```

Connection from Node.js (as used in this project)

```javascript
// src/storage/kb-embed.js pattern
const { Pool } = require('pg');

const pool = new Pool({
  host: process.env.RUVECTOR_HOST || 'localhost',
  port: parseInt(process.env.RUVECTOR_PORT || '5435'),
  database: 'postgres',
  user: 'postgres',
  // Password via ~/.pgpass (never inline!)
});

// Insert with pre-computed ONNX embedding
async function insertDocument(doc) {
  const embedding = await embed(doc.title + ' ' + doc.content);
  const vecStr = '[' + embedding.join(',') + ']';
  
  await pool.query(`
    INSERT INTO private_rag.documents (title, content, source, embedding)
    VALUES ($1, $2, $3, $4::ruvector(384))
    ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE
    SET content = EXCLUDED.content, embedding = EXCLUDED.embedding
  `, [doc.title, doc.content, doc.source, vecStr]);
}

// Search
async function searchDocuments(query, limit = 10) {
  const embedding = await embed(query);
  const vecStr = '[' + embedding.join(',') + ']';
  
  const result = await pool.query(`
    SELECT id, title, content,
           1 - (embedding <=> $1::ruvector(384)) AS similarity
    FROM private_rag.documents
    ORDER BY embedding <=> $1::ruvector(384)
    LIMIT $2
  `, [vecStr, limit]);
  
  return result.rows;
}
```

The 77+ SQL functions in RuVector PostgreSQL include: ruvector_embed(), ruvector_cosine(), ruvector_l2(), ruvector_normalize(), ruvector_quantize(), and graph traversal functions like ruvector_neighbors() and ruvector_cluster().

title: RVF Cognitive Container: Single-File Private AI Deployment
content:
The RuVector Format (RVF) is a binary file format that packages vectors, models, WASM runtime, kernel, and audit trail into a single deployable file. It is the most streamlined path to private AI deployment.

What an RVF File Contains

An RVF file uses 20 segment types:
- VEC: Raw vector data (compressed Float32 embeddings)
- INDEX: HNSW index structure
- WASM: 5.5 KB WASM runtime for browser queries
- KERNEL: Bootable Linux microkernel (for self-hosting)
- EBPF: XDP/TC programs for hardware-speed lookups
- COW_MAP: Copy-on-write branching metadata
- WITNESS: Cryptographic audit chain
- CRYPTO: Post-quantum signing keys (ML-DSA-65, Ed25519)
- MODEL: GGUF model weights (optional, for complete AI in one file)

The RVF file can be as small as a vector index (a few MB) or as large as a complete AI system with a 7B model (~4 GB).

Creating an RVF File

```bash
# Install RVF CLI
cargo install rvf-cli
# or via npm
npm install @ruvector/rvf

# Pack an existing vector store into RVF
rvf-cli pack \
  --vectors .ruvector/knowledge-base/vectors.bin \
  --metadata .ruvector/knowledge-base/metadata.json \
  --dimensions 384 \
  --output private-knowledge.rvf

# Add a model (complete private AI in one file)
rvf-cli pack \
  --vectors .ruvector/knowledge-base/vectors.bin \
  --model ~/.ollama/models/llama3.1-8b.Q4_K_M.gguf \
  --wasm-runtime embedded \
  --output complete-ai.rvf  # ~5 GB total

# Sign for tamper-proof distribution
rvf-cli sign complete-ai.rvf \
  --algorithm ed25519 \
  --key signing.key
```

Deploying an RVF as a Microservice

```bash
# Copy single file to any Linux server
scp complete-ai.rvf prod@server:/opt/ai/

# Boot as microservice (125ms startup time)
rvf-cli run /opt/ai/complete-ai.rvf \
  --port 8080 \
  --network isolated  # No external internet

# The running microservice exposes:
# GET  /health         - Health check
# POST /search         - Vector search
# POST /query          - RAG query (uses embedded model)
# GET  /audit          - Tamper-evident query log
```

Running in a Browser (5.5 KB WASM runtime)

```javascript
import { RVFRuntime } from '@ruvector/rvf-wasm';

// Load the RVF file (vector-only version, no model)
const response = await fetch('/private-knowledge.rvf');
const rvfData = await response.arrayBuffer();

const runtime = new RVFRuntime();
await runtime.load(rvfData);

// All queries run in browser, zero backend needed
const results = await runtime.search({
  query: 'What is the refund policy?',
  k: 5,
  embedLocally: true  // Uses wasm-embedded all-MiniLM model
});

console.log(results); // Returned from browser WASM, no server call
```

Git-Like Branching for Multi-Tenant Deployments

COW (Copy-on-Write) branching lets you create lightweight copies of a knowledge base for different departments without duplicating all the data:

```bash
# 1M vector base knowledge base
rvf-cli create-branch \
  --source company-kb.rvf \
  --name hr-department.rvf
# Creates ~2.5 MB child (only stores the differences)

# Add HR-specific documents to the branch
rvf-cli insert hr-department.rvf ./hr-policies/*.pdf

# Deploy different branches to different teams
rvf-cli run hr-department.rvf --port 8081  # HR team
rvf-cli run legal-department.rvf --port 8082  # Legal team
# Each team only sees their own + shared knowledge
```

Witness Chain for Compliance

Every operation on an RVF file creates a hash-linked audit entry:

```bash
# View audit trail for compliance reporting
rvf-cli audit-log private-knowledge.rvf \
  --from 2026-01-01 \
  --format json \
  --output audit-2026-q1.json

# Verify chain integrity (detect tampering)
rvf-cli verify-chain private-knowledge.rvf
# Output: "Witness chain intact. 4,521 verified operations."

# Each entry contains:
# - operation: INSERT | QUERY | DELETE
# - timestamp (millisecond precision)
# - actor: user ID
# - content hash: SHA-256 of the document
# - chain hash: links to previous entry
# - signature: Ed25519 or ML-DSA-65
```

This makes the RVF format suitable for HIPAA audit trail requirements, financial transaction records, and legal document provenance chains without any external audit database.

title: SONA Self-Optimizing Neural Architecture in Private Deployment
content:
SONA (Self-Optimizing Neural Architecture) is RuVector's adaptive learning system that improves routing and retrieval quality over time. In private deployments, all SONA learning stays local.

What SONA Does

SONA watches how your RAG system performs and continuously adjusts:
- Which retrieval strategies produce relevant results for which query types
- How to weight semantic vs keyword search for your domain
- Which model produces better outputs for specific question categories
- When to escalate to a larger (slower) model vs use a smaller (faster) one

Core SONA Components

1. LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation): Lightweight fine-tuning that adapts the embedding model to your domain vocabulary without full retraining
   - Rank-2 Micro-LoRA: 2,211 ops/sec, minimal memory (<5 MB adapter)
   - Rank-16 Base-LoRA: +55% quality improvement for specialised domains

2. EWC++ (Elastic Weight Consolidation): Prevents catastrophic forgetting. When SONA learns a new domain, it does not forget patterns from previous domains. Lambda 2000-2500 is optimal.

3. HNSW-indexed pattern store: SONA stores learned routing patterns as vectors in a local HNSW index, enabling 150x-12,500x faster pattern retrieval vs naive storage.

4. Self-adaptation latency: <0.05ms per adaptation cycle (does not add perceptible latency to queries).

Using SONA in Ruflo V3

```bash
# Initialize SONA neural training on your codebase
npx ruflo@latest hooks pretrain \
  --model-type moe \
  --epochs 10

# Train on successful task patterns
npx ruflo@latest neural train \
  --pattern-type coordination \
  --epochs 10

# View learned patterns
npx ruflo@latest neural patterns --list

# Predict optimal approach for a new task
npx ruflo@latest neural predict \
  --input "Analyze GDPR compliance of document corpus"
# Output: recommended agent, model, retrieval strategy
```

Storing Learning Patterns Locally

```bash
# Store a successful RAG pattern for future reference
npx ruflo@latest memory store \
  --key "legal-rag-pattern" \
  --value "For legal documents: use hybrid search, efSearch=100, top-k=8, mistral:7b" \
  --namespace patterns

# Retrieve pattern for next legal query
npx ruflo@latest memory search \
  --query "legal document retrieval" \
  --namespace patterns
```

ReasoningBank: Trajectory Learning

The ReasoningBank (used in RuvectorStore.reflexion) stores complete task trajectories:

```javascript
// Store a successful RAG interaction
await reasoningBank.store(
  queryText,           // The input
  {
    task: 'rag-query',
    output: answer,
    reward: userRating, // 0.0-1.0, from thumbs up/down or automated eval
    success: true,
    critique: 'Good retrieval but response could be more concise',
    latencyMs: responseTime
  }
);

// Later: retrieve similar past trajectories to guide current query
const similar = await reasoningBank.reflexion.retrieveRelevant({
  task: newQuery,
  k: 5  // Get 5 most similar past successful queries
});
```

The 4-Step Intelligence Pipeline

1. RETRIEVE: Fetch relevant past patterns via HNSW (150x-12,500x faster than brute-force)
2. JUDGE: Evaluate patterns by reward score (only high-reward patterns are reused)
3. DISTILL: Extract key learnings via LoRA (compress knowledge into the adapter)
4. CONSOLIDATE: Run EWC++ to prevent forgetting while integrating new knowledge

This cycle runs after every successful interaction and takes <0.05ms, making it invisible to end users.

MoE (Mixture of Experts) Routing

For multi-domain private RAG (e.g., legal + HR + technical docs), MoE routing assigns different expert models to different domains:

```javascript
// MoE configuration in Ruflo V3
{
  "routing": {
    "strategy": "moe",
    "experts": {
      "legal": { "model": "mistral:7b-instruct", "efSearch": 100 },
      "technical": { "model": "llama3.1:8b", "efSearch": 50 },
      "hr": { "model": "phi3:mini", "efSearch": 30 }
    },
    "classificationModel": "phi3:mini"  // Fast model for domain detection
  }
}
```

Privacy of SONA Learning

All SONA learning data is stored in local files:
- `.claude-flow/neural/patterns.json` - Learned neural patterns
- `.claude-flow/metrics/learning.json` - Training metrics
- `agentdb.db` - SQLite database with trajectory records
- `.claude-flow/memory/store.json` - Pattern memory

None of this data is transmitted to Anthropic or RuVector servers. The learning is entirely local.

title: Private AI Cost Analysis: On-Premises vs Cloud RAG Total Cost of Ownership
content:
Understanding the economics of private AI deployment helps justify the infrastructure investment. This provides a realistic TCO comparison for a mid-size enterprise deploying a private RAG system.

Scenario: 50-user enterprise document Q&A system, 100,000 documents, 1,000 queries per day.

Cloud RAG Stack (Annual Cost)

Embedding API (OpenAI text-embedding-3-small):
- Initial ingestion: 100,000 docs x 2,000 chars avg = 200M tokens = $4.00
- Daily queries: 1,000 queries x 200 token avg = 200K tokens = $0.004/day = $1.46/year
- Total embedding: ~$6/year (negligible)

Vector Database (Pinecone standard tier):
- 100,000 vectors: $70/month = $840/year
- With metadata and high availability: $150-200/month = $2,400/year

LLM Generation (Claude Sonnet API):
- 1,000 queries/day x 3,000 tokens input avg x $3/M = $9/day = $3,285/year
- 1,000 queries/day x 500 tokens output avg x $15/M = $7.50/day = $2,738/year
- Total LLM: ~$6,023/year

Cloud RAG Annual Total: ~$9,000-10,000/year
Compliance overhead: DPA negotiations, security reviews, cloud audits: $5,000-20,000/year
Risk: Data breach at vendor, pricing changes, API deprecations

Private RAG Stack with RuVector (Total Cost of Ownership)

Hardware (one-time, amortised over 3 years):
- Dedicated server: Apple Mac Mini M4 Pro (48GB RAM): $2,000
- Or: Dell server with NVIDIA RTX 4080: $4,000-6,000
- Amortised: $667-2,000/year

Electricity:
- Mac Mini M4 Pro: 20W idle, 40W under load
- 8 hours/day load: 40W x 8h x 365 = 116 kWh = $17/year
- Total electricity: ~$17-50/year

Software:
- RuVector: MIT license, $0
- Ollama: Free, $0
- Ruflo V3: npx, $0
- Maintenance time: 2 hours/month x $100/hr = $2,400/year (if outsourced)

Private RAG Annual Total: $3,000-5,000/year (all-in)
Compliance overhead: No vendor DPA needed, existing security controls apply: ~$1,000/year
Risk: Hardware failure (mitigated by backups), internal maintenance burden

Break-Even Analysis

Cloud cost at 1,000 queries/day: ~$9,000/year
Private cost (hardware amortised + electricity + maintenance): ~$4,000/year
Savings: $5,000/year
Hardware payback period: 2,000 / 5,000 = 0.4 years (less than 5 months)

At scale (10,000 queries/day):
- Cloud: ~$70,000/year
- Private: ~$8,000/year (more hardware, but linear scaling)
- Savings: $62,000/year

Hidden Costs Often Overlooked in Cloud

1. Egress fees: Downloading data from cloud = $0.09/GB (AWS). 100GB/month = $108/year.
2. Premium support: $10,000-100,000/year for enterprise SLAs
3. Multi-region replication: 2x-3x base cost
4. GDPR DPA legal review: $5,000-20,000 one-time
5. SOC 2 audit for vendor: Time cost of annual vendor security reviews
6. Lock-in: Migrating from cloud vector DB costs months of engineering time

When Cloud Makes More Sense

- Prototyping (no hardware purchase needed)
- Highly variable load (burst to cloud)
- Very small query volume (<100 queries/day: hardware cost not justified)
- Teams without ops capacity to maintain servers
- Regulatory environments that require certified cloud providers (FedRAMP)

RuVector Hybrid Architecture

For organisations that want the best of both: run private for sensitive data, cloud for non-sensitive:

```javascript
// Route sensitive queries to private stack, others to cloud
const router = {
  async route(query, document) {
    if (document.classification === 'confidential' || 
        document.containsPII) {
      return privateRAG(query, document);  // Local Ollama + RuVector
    } else {
      return cloudRAG(query, document);    // Claude API + Pinecone
    }
  }
};
```

title: Flash Attention in RuVector: What It Is and When It Applies
content:
Flash Attention is frequently cited in RuVector and Ruflo V3 documentation with 2.49x-7.47x speedup numbers. This clarifies exactly what is being accelerated, under what conditions, and how to use it.

What Flash Attention Accelerates

Flash Attention accelerates the multi-head self-attention computation inside transformer models. It does this by restructuring the computation to be memory-bandwidth-efficient: rather than materialising the full N x N attention matrix (which grows quadratically with sequence length), it computes attention in tiles that fit in fast SRAM/cache.

What Flash Attention Does NOT Accelerate

- Vector similarity search (HNSW handles that, ~125x speedup)
- Embedding generation throughput (ONNX handles that, ~55x speedup)
- The LLM token generation rate directly (though it helps with long contexts)
- Anything outside transformer attention layers

Where the 2.49x-7.47x Numbers Apply

These numbers come from the Agentic-Flow performance analysis and apply to multi-head attention in the agent coordination layer:

```
Configuration: 8 heads, 64d, seq=128
- Original attention: 177.814ms avg
- Flash Attention: 101.475ms avg
- Speedup: 1.75x (in this benchmark)

FlashAttention benchmark:
- Original: 121.232ms avg  
- Optimized: 67.400ms avg
- Speedup: 1.80x
- Throughput: 17 passes/sec
```

The 2.49x (JavaScript) and 7.47x (NAPI) figures are for specific sequence lengths and head configurations in the Ruflo V3 attention coordinator:

```javascript
// Flash Attention usage in Ruflo V3 agent coordination
const { FlashAttention } = require('@claude-flow/agentdb');

const flashAttn = new FlashAttention({
  numHeads: 8,
  headDim: 64,
  seqLen: 128    // Agent context window
});

// Coordinate multiple agent outputs via attention
const consensus = await flashAttn.forward(
  agentQueryStates,   // Q: what each agent is looking for
  agentKeyStates,     // K: what each agent has found
  agentValueStates    // V: agent outputs to combine
);
```

Flash Attention for Large Document Processing

For processing large document sets (50+ documents), Flash Attention reduces memory from O(n^2) to O(n):

```javascript
// From CLAUDE.md: use Flash Attention for large document sets
if (documentCount > 50) {
  const result = await agentDB.flashAttention(
    queryEmbedding,
    documentEmbeddings,
    documentEmbeddings
  );
  // Speed improvement: 2.49x-7.47x faster
  // Memory reduction: ~50%
}
```

Runtime Selection (NAPI vs WASM vs JS)

Ruflo V3 automatically selects the best available Flash Attention runtime:

```
Runtime selection priority:
1. NAPI (native Node.js bindings via napi-rs): 7.47x speedup
   - Available when: running in Node.js with native compilation supported
   - Latency: <0.1ms for all operations

2. WASM SIMD: ~3-5x speedup  
   - Available when: browser or Node.js without native bindings
   - Latency: <0.1ms

3. JavaScript fallback: 1x baseline
   - Available always
   - Latency: <0.1ms (still fast for agent coordination sizes)
```

Ollama and Flash Attention

Ollama 0.3+ enables Flash Attention in llama.cpp for the LLM inference itself:

```bash
export OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1
ollama serve

# Effect on long-context requests (8K+ tokens):
# Without FA: ~580ms time-to-first-token
# With FA: ~320ms time-to-first-token (~1.8x speedup)
# Memory: 30-50% reduction for long contexts
```

For the agent orchestration layer in Ruflo V3, Flash Attention is enabled by default in v3.0.0-alpha.118+ and requires no explicit configuration.

title: Enterprise WASM Model Deployment: Patterns for Regulated Industries
All-MiniLM-L6-v2: The Embedding Model Behind Private RuVector Deployments
content:
All-MiniLM-L6-v2 is the default embedding model used throughout the Ask-Ruvnet codebase and RuVector deployments. Understanding it helps you make informed decisions about embedding quality and alternatives.

Model Specifications

- Architecture: Sentence-BERT (SBERT) with MiniLM distillation
- Parameters: 22.7 million
- Output dimensions: 384 (used throughout this codebase)
- Maximum input: 256 tokens (approximately 200 words)
- Model size on disk: 22 MB (ONNX format), 45 MB (PyTorch)
- License: Apache 2.0 (free for commercial private use)
- Source: Microsoft Research -> HuggingFace (Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2)

Why This Model for Private RAG

1. Size: 22 MB ONNX model vs 1.5 GB for larger models. Downloads once, cached locally.
2. Speed: 930 embeds/sec with ONNX on Apple M3 Max (for @claude-flow/embeddings)
3. Quality: Ranks in top 5 on MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark) for its size class
4. License: Commercial use allowed, no attribution required
5. Offline: Once cached, never calls HuggingFace servers

Throughput Comparison (from this codebase, src/storage/kb-embed.js)

| Method | Throughput | Notes |
|--------|-----------|-------|
| ONNX via @claude-flow/embeddings | ~930 embeds/sec | Primary path |
| PostgreSQL ruvector_embed() | ~17 embeds/sec | Fallback |
| @xenova/transformers | ~50-100 embeds/sec | Secondary fallback |
| OpenAI API | ~500 embeds/sec (network dependent) | Cloud, not private |

Single embed latency: ~1ms with ONNX (vs ~3-5ms with @xenova/transformers).

Model Download and Caching

```javascript
// Model is downloaded once to local cache
const svc = await createEmbeddingServiceAsync({
  provider: 'transformers',
  model: 'Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2',
  dimensions: 384
});

// Cache location:
// Linux/Mac: ~/.cache/huggingface/hub/models--Xenova--all-MiniLM-L6-v2/
// Windows: C:\Users\username\.cache\huggingface\hub\

// For air-gapped deployment, pre-cache before disconnecting:
export TRANSFORMERS_OFFLINE=1
export TRANSFORMERS_CACHE=/opt/models/huggingface
```

Alternative Embedding Models for Different Needs

Smaller (faster, less accurate):
- all-MiniLM-L3-v2: 17M params, 384 dims, ~1.3x faster, slightly less accurate
- TinyBERT: 14.5M params, 128 dims, very fast on edge devices

Larger (slower, more accurate):
- all-mpnet-base-v2: 110M params, 768 dims, better for technical/specialized content
- text-embedding-3-small (OpenAI): 1536 dims, excellent quality but not local
- e5-base-v2: 109M params, 768 dims, strong for retrieval tasks

Domain-Specific Alternatives:
- legal-bert-base-uncased: Pre-trained on legal documents (better for legal RAG)
- bioBERT: Pre-trained on medical literature (better for healthcare RAG)
- CodeBERT: Pre-trained on code (better for software documentation RAG)

Dimension Choice and RuVector Configuration

All-MiniLM-L6-v2 produces 384-dimensional vectors. The RuvectorStore and PersistentVectorDB are both configured for 384 dimensions:

```javascript
// From src/core/RuvectorStore.js
this.dimensions = 384;  // Standardized to all-MiniLM-L6-v2

// From src/storage/kb-embed.js
const MAX_TEXT_LEN = 2000;  // Truncated before embedding (model max is ~1000 chars)
```

If you switch to a different embedding model with different dimensions, you must:
1. Update dimensions in PersistentVectorDB config
2. Rebuild (re-embed) all existing vectors (incompatible dimensions)
3. Update the HNSW index dimensions

Never mix vectors from different models in the same index.
